name: cable palm rotational row
instructions: Attach a handle to a cable machine at waist height.
Stand facing the machine with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Grasp the handle with an overhand grip, palms facing down.
Step back to create tension on the cable, keeping your back straight and knees slightly bent.
Pull the handle towards your body, rotating your palms to face upwards as you do so.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of the movement.
Slowly release the handle back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Attach a handle to a cable machine at waist height. Stand facing the machine with your feet shoulder-width apart. Grasp the handle with an overhand grip, palms facing down. Step back to create tension on the cable, keeping your back straight and knees slightly bent. Pull the handle towards your body, rotating your palms to face upwards as you do so. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of the movement. Slowly release the handle back to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Favor symmetrical loading and supported variations if fatigue changes your posture.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Avoid loaded spinal flexion and excessive hinge depth. Brace core and use supported positions.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
